She was twelve years old and sat in a class with six-year-olds. She could not say the word "the." She could not say "water" — only "vater." But Mary Antin had come all the way from Russia, and she was not going to stop now. Her teacher did not give up on her. And she did not give up on herself. Four months after arriving in America, she wrote an essay about snow. Her teacher sent it to a journal. And one afternoon, after school, the teacher showed her something impossible: her own name, in print, at the bottom of a real published page.
